i.Contents

  2. Audit opinion & sign-off

1.Engagement summary

1.1 · Objective

The objective of this engagement is to provide the Audit Committee, the Chief Financial Officer, and the Internal Audit function with an independent, evidence-based assessment of the control environment over the customer RPS01 folder of the Sage X3 ERP. The audit evaluates the design adequacy and operating effectiveness of key financial, operational, and IT general controls across eight process areas.

1.2 · Scope

The audit covers 74 control objectives, each instantiated as a parameterised T-SQL view catalogued in the knowledge graph table kb_role_view with role='auditor'. The control objectives span:

  • Procure-to-Pay (P2P) — purchase orders, three-way match, AP invoices, vendor master, cutoff testing
  • Order-to-Cash (O2C) — sales orders, deliveries, AR invoices, customer master, price overrides
  • Record-to-Report (R2R) — manual journals, chart of accounts, AR ageing, period close, FX revaluation
  • Fixed Assets (FA) — register completeness and disposals
  • Audit Trail (AUDITH) — change-trail completeness across critical financial tables
  • IT General Controls (ITGC) — user-access hygiene, SoD, profiles, customisations, login audit
  • Inventory — stock balances, item master, lot & movement integrity
  • Manufacturing — work orders, materials, operations, BOM

1.3 · Methodology

For each control objective the audit executed the corresponding T-SQL view against the live MSSQL RPS01 database, capturing the full population row count and a sampled evidence trail (top N rows, default 200). Each result was evaluated against a registered interpretation rule (scripts/mvp5/audit_findings_rules.py) — a deterministic ruleset encoding severity logic, control criteria, likely cause, business effect, and remediation recommendations. High-value findings are further enriched with full-population legal-entity drill-down queries against the underlying source tables (scripts/mvp5/audit_enrichment.py).

Sample-size determination follows ISA 530 — for routine populations > 250 a sample of 25 supports substantive procedures; for high-risk populations (manual journals, closed-period postings, vendor duplicates) the report recommends 100% review.

1.4 · Limitations and inherent caveats

  • Findings are derived from on-disk transactional data. The audit did not test the validity of underlying business documentation (invoices, contracts, delivery notes).
  • Segregation-of-duties coverage is bounded by the rules currently encoded in V_ITGC_SOD_FLAGS; the auditor should confirm the matrix encompasses the ≥15 standard high-risk pairs.
  • Period-cutoff testing is based on the gap between accounting_date and created_on; corroboration with physical evidence (shipping documents, customer acknowledgement) is the responsibility of the auditor.
  • Interpretation rules in the registered ruleset are first-pass and reflect auditor heuristics; they have not been independently validated by an external SME.
  • The audit does not opine on financial-statement accuracy; the findings inform but do not substitute for the financial-statement audit performed under ISA 700.

2.Audit opinion & sign-off

2.1 · Statement of opinion

In our opinion, based on the procedures performed and the evidence obtained, the internal control environment over the RPS01 folder of the Sage X3 ERP deployment receives a Qualified opinion. This determination is reached because the audit identified 5 HIGH and 28 MEDIUM exception(s) reflecting material control weaknesses in scoped areas. Specific findings, remediation recommendations and proposed timelines are documented in Section 6 of this report.

This opinion is rendered on a control-by-control basis derived from 111 scored control tests (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/CLEAN). An additional 0 reference / population datasets are documented in Appendix D — they provide the evidence base the scored controls sample from, but are not themselves control tests with a pass/fail verdict and therefore do not influence this opinion. This engagement does not substitute for a Statement of Internal Control under SOX §404 nor for a financial-statement audit opinion under ISA 700; it is intended to support the audit committee, management, and the external auditor in their respective responsibilities.

Lead auditor
Signature · Date
Reviewed by — Audit Committee
Signature · Date

3.3 · Findings requiring immediate management attention

  • HIGH F-ITGC-003 — Disabled account still flagged as super-user · IT General Controls · 53 row(s)
  • HIGH F-P2P-001 — Duplicate vendor invoice (same supplier, vendor doc reference and amount) · Procure-to-Pay · 41 row(s)
  • HIGH F-ITGC-001 — Sensitive security/config table without change auditing enabled · IT General Controls · 6 row(s)
  • HIGH F-ITGC-002 — AVALNUM counter is zero or negative · IT General Controls · 2 row(s)
  • HIGH F-R2R-001 — Reversal must point to an existing original document · Record-to-Report · 1 row(s)

4.Materiality framework

The audit applies a hybrid quantitative-and-qualitative materiality framework consistent with ISA 320 and the firm's professional judgment. Severity tiering at finding level draws on both the population magnitude and the qualitative nature of the control objective.

4.1 · Quantitative thresholds (overall materiality)

BenchmarkThresholdApplies to
Profit before tax5%Income-statement items
Total revenue0.5% – 1%Revenue / AR exceptions
Total assets1%Inventory / FA / cash
Equity3%Adjustments & restatements
Performance materiality50% – 75% of overallSampling-tolerance threshold
Trivial threshold5% of performanceAggregation floor

4.2 · Qualitative considerations

FactorTreatment
Fraud riskAlways material (ISA 240 §10)
Regulatory non-complianceMaterial if disclosable
Related-party transactionsMaterial regardless of value
Period-close integrityMaterial — see SOX §404
SoD breachesMaterial — SOX entity-level control
Audit-trail completenessMaterial — ISA 315 risk assessment

4.3 · Severity mapping

TierDescriptionAction expected
CRITICALMaterial control failure; significant deficiency or material weakness under SOX §404Immediate remediation; Audit Committee notification
HIGHMaterial control weakness; aggregated impact above performance materialityRemediation within current quarter
MEDIUMProcess improvement; below performance materiality individually but trendingRemediation within current fiscal year
LOWMinor observation; process-hygiene matterBest-effort improvement
CLEANNo exception detected on this dimensionContinue monitoring; reperform next cycle
INFOReference data or population sizing; not a findingUse as evidence base for related findings

6.Detailed findings Scored controls · click any finding to expand

This section presents the 111 scored control tests in descending severity. Each finding follows a standardised structure: Observation → Criteria → Cause → Effect → Detailed analysis (legal-entity drill-down with amounts) → Recommendation → Management response. The Risk-rating bar at the top of each finding shows Inherent risk, Control effectiveness, Residual risk, and the ISA-315 audit assertions tested. The 0 reference / population datasets that feed these controls are documented separately in Appendix D.

No findings match the current filter

Clear the filter or select a different severity / area.

F-ITGC-001

Sensitive security/config table without change auditing enabled

HIGH IT General Controls Source: SY-F10-001 6 row(s) tested · sample 8
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Weak
Residual risk High
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

6 exception(s) detected. All 7 highest-sensitivity control tables (users, profiles, access codes, roles, parameters) have audit flags OFF in this folder. Changes to who-can-do-what are NOT captured in AUDITH — auditors cannot answer 'who granted this access and when'. Watch-list is auditor-supplied..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Sensitive changes must be logged and attributable (audit-trail completeness; SOX ITGC).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A sensitive table lacks change auditing, or a change is not attributable to a known user.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Without an audit trail, unauthorised changes cannot be detected or investigated.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Enable change auditing on the sensitive tables and ensure every change is attributable to an authenticated user.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerChief Information Officer
SupportingIT Security Lead · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceMonthly until cleared · then semi-annual
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 14 days Triage the full population by amount and recency; investigate the top 25 items within the timeline. Implement compensating manual controls (4-eyes review, daily reporting) on the affected process until the root-cause remediation is in place.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 60 days Enable change auditing on the sensitive tables and ensure every change is attributable to an authenticated user.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 120 days Enforce 90-day password rotation OR (preferred) adopt MFA + SAML SSO; configure automatic deactivation of accounts after 90 days of inactivity; document and operationalise the SoD matrix covering ≥ 15 high-risk pairs; quarterly access-rights attestation by line managers with archived evidence.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Monthly until cleared · then semi-annual Zero dormant active accounts; SoD matrix coverage ≥ 15 high-risk pairs with zero matrix-violation user-rights records; password / MFA compliance ≥ 99% on the active user population.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

9 · Reproduction

SELECT * FROM CUST.ATABLE;
10 · Sample evidence — 8 of 6 rows (click to expand)
F-ITGC-002

AVALNUM counter is zero or negative

HIGH IT General Controls Source: SY-F7-007 2 row(s) tested · sample 2
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Weak
Residual risk High
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

2 exception(s) detected. A zero/negative counter means the next allocation will be 1 (or negative), re-issuing already-used document numbers — a guaranteed duplicate. Cheap invariant, high consequence..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Document sequences must be gap-free and duplicate-free (completeness assertion; a primary fraud signal).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A gap or duplicate appeared in a document number sequence.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

A missing document may be unrecorded (completeness) or deleted (override); a duplicate may be double-counted.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Investigate each gap/duplicate, confirm the missing documents, and lock the numbering counter against manual edit.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerChief Information Officer
SupportingIT Security Lead · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceMonthly until cleared · then semi-annual
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 14 days Triage the full population by amount and recency; investigate the top 25 items within the timeline. Implement compensating manual controls (4-eyes review, daily reporting) on the affected process until the root-cause remediation is in place.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 60 days Investigate each gap/duplicate, confirm the missing documents, and lock the numbering counter against manual edit.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 120 days Enforce 90-day password rotation OR (preferred) adopt MFA + SAML SSO; configure automatic deactivation of accounts after 90 days of inactivity; document and operationalise the SoD matrix covering ≥ 15 high-risk pairs; quarterly access-rights attestation by line managers with archived evidence.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Monthly until cleared · then semi-annual Zero dormant active accounts; SoD matrix coverage ≥ 15 high-risk pairs with zero matrix-violation user-rights records; password / MFA compliance ≥ 99% on the active user population.

F-ITGC-003

Disabled account still flagged as super-user

HIGH IT General Controls Source: SY-F8-003 53 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Weak
Residual risk High
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

53 exception(s) detected. An inactive account retaining all-access rights is a latent privilege-escalation target: if re-enabled it instantly holds super-user power. Privilege revocation should accompany deactivation..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Incompatible duties must be segregated and access appropriately restricted (COSO Principle 10/11; SOX ITGC).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A user holds an incompatible combination of rights, or an over-privileged / orphaned account exists.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

A single individual can both initiate and conceal a transaction — the core fraud-enablement condition.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Remove the conflicting access or add a compensating review; disable orphaned/all-access accounts; re-certify access.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerChief Information Officer
SupportingIT Security Lead · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceMonthly until cleared · then semi-annual
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 14 days Triage the full population by amount and recency; investigate the top 25 items within the timeline. Implement compensating manual controls (4-eyes review, daily reporting) on the affected process until the root-cause remediation is in place.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 60 days Remove the conflicting access or add a compensating review; disable orphaned/all-access accounts; re-certify access.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 120 days Enforce 90-day password rotation OR (preferred) adopt MFA + SAML SSO; configure automatic deactivation of accounts after 90 days of inactivity; document and operationalise the SoD matrix covering ≥ 15 high-risk pairs; quarterly access-rights attestation by line managers with archived evidence.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Monthly until cleared · then semi-annual Zero dormant active accounts; SoD matrix coverage ≥ 15 high-risk pairs with zero matrix-violation user-rights records; password / MFA compliance ≥ 99% on the active user population.

F-P2P-001

Duplicate vendor invoice (same supplier, vendor doc reference and amount)

HIGH Procure-to-Pay Source: PU-F7-001 41 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Weak
Residual risk High
Audit assertions tested Completeness Cutoff Valuation/Accuracy Rights & Obligations

1 · Observation

41 exception(s) detected. Same supplier document number and amount entered twice is the canonical duplicate-payment risk. SNS=1 restricts to invoices (excludes credit notes)..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Document sequences must be gap-free and duplicate-free (completeness assertion; a primary fraud signal).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A gap or duplicate appeared in a document number sequence.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

A missing document may be unrecorded (completeness) or deleted (override); a duplicate may be double-counted.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Investigate each gap/duplicate, confirm the missing documents, and lock the numbering counter against manual edit.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerHead of Procurement
SupportingAP Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceMonthly until cleared · then semi-annual
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 14 days Triage the full population by amount and recency; investigate the top 25 items within the timeline. Implement compensating manual controls (4-eyes review, daily reporting) on the affected process until the root-cause remediation is in place.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 60 days Investigate each gap/duplicate, confirm the missing documents, and lock the numbering counter against manual edit.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 120 days Lock down the upstream control: enforce vendor-onboarding due diligence (VIES VAT-ID lookup, bank-account verification with callback, AML / sanctions screening) at the parameter level. Configure the X3 workflow to require 4-eyes approval on every PO above the materiality threshold and to block self-approval. Enable the duplicate-invoice-number check (INVCTLREF=block).
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Monthly until cleared · then semi-annual Exception volume reduced by ≥ 50% within 90 days; reached industry-baseline tolerance within 12 months. KPI tracked monthly with trend chart presented to Audit Committee quarterly.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

9 · Reproduction

SELECT * FROM CUST.PINVOICE;
10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 41 rows (click to expand)
F-R2R-001

Reversal must point to an existing original document

HIGH Record-to-Report Source: GL-F10-002 1 row(s) tested · sample 1
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Weak
Residual risk High
Audit assertions tested Existence Completeness Accuracy Cutoff Classification Presentation

1 · Observation

1 exception(s) detected. A reversal referencing a non-existent original is a dangling audit link — the supposed reversed entry cannot be inspected..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Sensitive changes must be logged and attributable (audit-trail completeness; SOX ITGC).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A sensitive table lacks change auditing, or a change is not attributable to a known user.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Without an audit trail, unauthorised changes cannot be detected or investigated.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Enable change auditing on the sensitive tables and ensure every change is attributable to an authenticated user.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerGroup Controller / CFO
SupportingFinancial Reporting Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceMonthly until cleared · then semi-annual
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 14 days Triage the full population by amount and recency; investigate the top 25 items within the timeline. Implement compensating manual controls (4-eyes review, daily reporting) on the affected process until the root-cause remediation is in place.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 60 days Enable change auditing on the sensitive tables and ensure every change is attributable to an authenticated user.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 120 days Strengthen period-close discipline: configure automatic period-lock at end-of-month + 5 working days; require dual-approval for any period reopen with documented business reason; route every manual JE above materiality through a 4-eyes approval workflow; weekly suspense / clearing account reconciliation with aged > 30-day escalation.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Monthly until cleared · then semi-annual Zero closed-period postings in next cycle; trial balance foots daily; period-close completes within 5 working days of month-end. TB-balance check automated as part of close checklist.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

10 · Sample evidence — 1 of 1 rows (click to expand)
F-ITGC-004

Audit-history row not attributed to a known X3 user

MEDIUM IT General Controls Source: SY-F10-005 34,166 row(s) tested · sample 0
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

34,166 exception(s) detected. An audit trail whose actor can't be resolved to a real user is not a usable accountability record. Extends kb_constraint #106.. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Sensitive changes must be logged and attributable (audit-trail completeness; SOX ITGC).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A sensitive table lacks change auditing, or a change is not attributable to a known user.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Without an audit trail, unauthorised changes cannot be detected or investigated.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Enable change auditing on the sensitive tables and ensure every change is attributable to an authenticated user.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerChief Information Officer
SupportingIT Security Lead · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Enable change auditing on the sensitive tables and ensure every change is attributable to an authenticated user.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Enforce 90-day password rotation OR (preferred) adopt MFA + SAML SSO; configure automatic deactivation of accounts after 90 days of inactivity; document and operationalise the SoD matrix covering ≥ 15 high-risk pairs; quarterly access-rights attestation by line managers with archived evidence.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Zero dormant active accounts; SoD matrix coverage ≥ 15 high-risk pairs with zero matrix-violation user-rights records; password / MFA compliance ≥ 99% on the active user population.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

F-ITGC-005

AVALNUM counter references an undefined sequence (orphan counter)

MEDIUM IT General Controls Source: SY-F7-006 2 row(s) tested · sample 2
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

2 exception(s) detected. A counter for a sequence code no longer in the dictionary is dangling state: the formatting rules that govern it are gone, so the next number is unpredictable..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Document sequences must be gap-free and duplicate-free (completeness assertion; a primary fraud signal).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A gap or duplicate appeared in a document number sequence.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

A missing document may be unrecorded (completeness) or deleted (override); a duplicate may be double-counted.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Investigate each gap/duplicate, confirm the missing documents, and lock the numbering counter against manual edit.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

F-ITGC-006

Gap in a contiguous, non-resetting document numbering scope

MEDIUM IT General Controls Source: SY-F7-009 978 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

978 exception(s) detected. Numbering gaps are a primary fraud/completeness signal (a missing journal number can mean a posting was deleted). On RPS01 the 416 are inflated: partial extract + RIGHT(NUM_0,6) crosses period/year resets. Mechanics correct; in prod scope to one (CODNUM,site,period) with known NIVRAZ_0 against a complete dataset..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Document sequences must be gap-free and duplicate-free (completeness assertion; a primary fraud signal).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A gap or duplicate appeared in a document number sequence.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

A missing document may be unrecorded (completeness) or deleted (override); a duplicate may be double-counted.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Investigate each gap/duplicate, confirm the missing documents, and lock the numbering counter against manual edit.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 978 rows (click to expand)
F-ITGC-007

Active user account holds no function profile

MEDIUM IT General Controls Source: SY-F8-001 16 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

16 exception(s) detected. A user with no function profile has access governed only by the blunt ALLACS_0 all-access flag (over-privilege) or is an enabled-but-unconfigured account (access-creep). Either way authorization is not explicitly scoped..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Incompatible duties must be segregated and access appropriately restricted (COSO Principle 10/11; SOX ITGC).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A user holds an incompatible combination of rights, or an over-privileged / orphaned account exists.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

A single individual can both initiate and conceal a transaction — the core fraud-enablement condition.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Remove the conflicting access or add a compensating review; disable orphaned/all-access accounts; re-certify access.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerChief Information Officer
SupportingIT Security Lead · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Remove the conflicting access or add a compensating review; disable orphaned/all-access accounts; re-certify access.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Enforce 90-day password rotation OR (preferred) adopt MFA + SAML SSO; configure automatic deactivation of accounts after 90 days of inactivity; document and operationalise the SoD matrix covering ≥ 15 high-risk pairs; quarterly access-rights attestation by line managers with archived evidence.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Zero dormant active accounts; SoD matrix coverage ≥ 15 high-risk pairs with zero matrix-violation user-rights records; password / MFA compliance ≥ 99% on the active user population.

F-ITGC-008

Active super-user (all-access flag) account

MEDIUM IT General Controls Source: SY-F8-002 108 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

108 exception(s) detected. 22 of 81 users (27%) carry the all-access super-user flag. Auditors expect super-user rights to be rare, named, justified, monitored. A high super-user ratio defeats the access-control model.. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Incompatible duties must be segregated and access appropriately restricted (COSO Principle 10/11; SOX ITGC).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A user holds an incompatible combination of rights, or an over-privileged / orphaned account exists.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

A single individual can both initiate and conceal a transaction — the core fraud-enablement condition.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Remove the conflicting access or add a compensating review; disable orphaned/all-access accounts; re-certify access.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

22 exception(s) detected. 20 of 58 function profiles carry the all-functions grant. Auditors expect least-privilege; a wide all-functions footprint means any such profile yields near-super-user power.. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Incompatible duties must be segregated and access appropriately restricted (COSO Principle 10/11; SOX ITGC).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A user holds an incompatible combination of rights, or an over-privileged / orphaned account exists.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

A single individual can both initiate and conceal a transaction — the core fraud-enablement condition.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Remove the conflicting access or add a compensating review; disable orphaned/all-access accounts; re-certify access.

10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 22 rows (click to expand)
F-ITGC-010

Sequence definition exists but has never been used (no counter)

MEDIUM IT General Controls Source: SY-F9-003 227 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

227 exception(s) detected. Informational census: 935 sequence defs have no live counter. In a real estate this separates genuinely-unused sequences from active ones, and is a useful denominator for the numbering controls. Info severity (most legitimately dormant in a partial dataset).. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Master data must be unique, complete and current (no duplicates, stale or dangling records).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

Duplicate, stale, or incomplete master records were detected.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Duplicate masters split spend/exposure and enable duplicate payment; stale records inflate the attack surface.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

De-duplicate and merge the masters, archive stale records, and add a duplicate check at creation.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerChief Information Officer
SupportingIT Security Lead · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly

Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation

3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Enforce 90-day password rotation OR (preferred) adopt MFA + SAML SSO; configure automatic deactivation of accounts after 90 days of inactivity; document and operationalise the SoD matrix covering ≥ 15 high-risk pairs; quarterly access-rights attestation by line managers with archived evidence.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Zero dormant active accounts; SoD matrix coverage ≥ 15 high-risk pairs with zero matrix-violation user-rights records; password / MFA compliance ≥ 99% on the active user population.

163 exception(s) detected. Broader duplicate-detection (ignores amount) — catches partial duplicates and split invoices reusing a vendor reference..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Document sequences must be gap-free and duplicate-free (completeness assertion; a primary fraud signal).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A gap or duplicate appeared in a document number sequence.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

A missing document may be unrecorded (completeness) or deleted (override); a duplicate may be double-counted.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Investigate each gap/duplicate, confirm the missing documents, and lock the numbering counter against manual edit.

10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 163 rows (click to expand)
F-P2P-003

Supplier must have a pay-to partner code

MEDIUM Procure-to-Pay Source: TP-F1-001 47 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Completeness Cutoff Valuation/Accuracy Rights & Obligations

1 · Observation

47 exception(s) detected. Without a pay-to partner the payment proposal cannot determine the payee — payments stall or default unexpectedly..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Mandatory fields and valid domain values are required for every record (data-integrity baseline; COSO Principle 13 — relevant, quality information).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

Records created or imported without the required field populated, or carrying a value outside the permitted domain.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Incomplete or invalid master/transaction data propagates downstream — mis-posting, failed interfaces, and unreliable reporting.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Make the field mandatory at entry, validate the domain, and remediate the existing exceptions.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerHead of Procurement
SupportingAP Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Make the field mandatory at entry, validate the domain, and remediate the existing exceptions.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Lock down the upstream control: enforce vendor-onboarding due diligence (VIES VAT-ID lookup, bank-account verification with callback, AML / sanctions screening) at the parameter level. Configure the X3 workflow to require 4-eyes approval on every PO above the materiality threshold and to block self-approval. Enable the duplicate-invoice-number check (INVCTLREF=block).
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Exception volume reduced by ≥ 50% within 90 days; reached industry-baseline tolerance within 12 months. KPI tracked monthly with trend chart presented to Audit Committee quarterly.

9 · Reproduction

SELECT * FROM CUST.BPSUPPLIER;
10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 47 rows (click to expand)
F-P2P-004

Customer must have a backing business-partner record

MEDIUM Procure-to-Pay Source: TP-F2-001 1,375 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Completeness Cutoff Valuation/Accuracy Rights & Obligations

1 · Observation

1,375 exception(s) detected. A customer with no BPARTNER row is a broken master inheritance — address, VAT, currency and control flags live on BPARTNER.. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Every foreign reference (third-party, account, parent document) must resolve to an existing master record (COSO Principle 10 — control activities over completeness).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A record references a master or parent that does not exist in the dataset — an orphan.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Orphans break reconciliations, hide liabilities/receivables, and indicate a broken create-sequence or an incomplete master file.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Enforce the foreign-key relationship; restore or create the missing master records; investigate the create process.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerHead of Procurement
SupportingAP Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Enforce the foreign-key relationship; restore or create the missing master records; investigate the create process.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Lock down the upstream control: enforce vendor-onboarding due diligence (VIES VAT-ID lookup, bank-account verification with callback, AML / sanctions screening) at the parameter level. Configure the X3 workflow to require 4-eyes approval on every PO above the materiality threshold and to block self-approval. Enable the duplicate-invoice-number check (INVCTLREF=block).
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Exception volume reduced by ≥ 50% within 90 days; reached industry-baseline tolerance within 12 months. KPI tracked monthly with trend chart presented to Audit Committee quarterly.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Target remediation date To be agreed

F-P2P-005

Supplier must have a backing business-partner record

MEDIUM Procure-to-Pay Source: TP-F2-002 6,849 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Completeness Cutoff Valuation/Accuracy Rights & Obligations

1 · Observation

6,849 exception(s) detected. A supplier with no BPARTNER row breaks master inheritance and orphans payment/banking data.. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Every foreign reference (third-party, account, parent document) must resolve to an existing master record (COSO Principle 10 — control activities over completeness).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A record references a master or parent that does not exist in the dataset — an orphan.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Orphans break reconciliations, hide liabilities/receivables, and indicate a broken create-sequence or an incomplete master file.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Enforce the foreign-key relationship; restore or create the missing master records; investigate the create process.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerHead of Procurement
SupportingAP Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Enforce the foreign-key relationship; restore or create the missing master records; investigate the create process.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Lock down the upstream control: enforce vendor-onboarding due diligence (VIES VAT-ID lookup, bank-account verification with callback, AML / sanctions screening) at the parameter level. Configure the X3 workflow to require 4-eyes approval on every PO above the materiality threshold and to block self-approval. Enable the duplicate-invoice-number check (INVCTLREF=block).
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Exception volume reduced by ≥ 50% within 90 days; reached industry-baseline tolerance within 12 months. KPI tracked monthly with trend chart presented to Audit Committee quarterly.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

9 · Reproduction

F-P2P-006

Supplier pay-to partner must exist in the partner master

MEDIUM Procure-to-Pay Source: TP-F2-003 6,802 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Completeness Cutoff Valuation/Accuracy Rights & Obligations

1 · Observation

6,802 exception(s) detected. Payments are issued to the pay-to partner (BPRPAY_0); if it does not exist, the payment run cannot resolve the payee — a direct disbursement risk.. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Every foreign reference (third-party, account, parent document) must resolve to an existing master record (COSO Principle 10 — control activities over completeness).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A record references a master or parent that does not exist in the dataset — an orphan.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Orphans break reconciliations, hide liabilities/receivables, and indicate a broken create-sequence or an incomplete master file.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Enforce the foreign-key relationship; restore or create the missing master records; investigate the create process.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

SELECT * FROM CUST.PAYMENTD;
10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 442,377 rows (click to expand)
F-P2P-011

Duplicate supplier by normalised name

MEDIUM Procure-to-Pay Source: TP-F9-001 226 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Completeness Cutoff Valuation/Accuracy Rights & Obligations

1 · Observation

226 exception(s) detected. Duplicate vendor masters split spend, enable duplicate payments, and defeat 3-way-match controls. 6 name collisions in RPS01 (e.g. APPLE x2, UNITED PARCEL SERVICE x2) warrant a merge review..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Master data must be unique, complete and current (no duplicates, stale or dangling records).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

Duplicate, stale, or incomplete master records were detected.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Duplicate masters split spend/exposure and enable duplicate payment; stale records inflate the attack surface.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

De-duplicate and merge the masters, archive stale records, and add a duplicate check at creation.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerHead of Procurement
SupportingAP Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days De-duplicate and merge the masters, archive stale records, and add a duplicate check at creation.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Lock down the upstream control: enforce vendor-onboarding due diligence (VIES VAT-ID lookup, bank-account verification with callback, AML / sanctions screening) at the parameter level. Configure the X3 workflow to require 4-eyes approval on every PO above the materiality threshold and to block self-approval. Enable the duplicate-invoice-number check (INVCTLREF=block).
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Exception volume reduced by ≥ 50% within 90 days; reached industry-baseline tolerance within 12 months. KPI tracked monthly with trend chart presented to Audit Committee quarterly.

Active supplier master not maintained within a year

▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Completeness Cutoff Valuation/Accuracy Rights & Obligations

1 · Observation

4,932 exception(s) detected. Stale active vendor records (bank details, addresses) are a payment-fraud and mis-payment risk. The RPS01 count is inflated by frozen demo dates against the current date — re-baseline on live data. Scoped to active suppliers (ENAFLG_0=2).. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Master data must be unique, complete and current (no duplicates, stale or dangling records).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

Duplicate, stale, or incomplete master records were detected.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Duplicate masters split spend/exposure and enable duplicate payment; stale records inflate the attack surface.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

De-duplicate and merge the masters, archive stale records, and add a duplicate check at creation.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

9 · Reproduction

SELECT * FROM CUST.BPSUPPLIER;
10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 4,932 rows (click to expand)
F-R2R-002

Legal-ledger line carrying an analytic key should have analytic detail (APPROXIMATE)

MEDIUM Record-to-Report Source: GL-F1-003 288,280 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Existence Completeness Accuracy Cutoff Classification Presentation

1 · Observation

288,280 exception(s) detected. Intended to catch dimension-bearing GL lines with no cost-accounting allocation. CHRNUM_0 (the internal analytic line id) proved to NOT be a reliable "analytic-required" signal — 4845 of 7885 CHRNUM-bearing legal lines (dominated by STOCK/CSDIR/PAYMT vouchers) legitimately have no CPTANALIN. The authoritative "analytic required" rule lives in GACCDIM (chart + account-mask → mandatory dimension); see Gaps. Keep as an exploratory signal only, not an enforced control.. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Mandatory fields and valid domain values are required for every record (data-integrity baseline; COSO Principle 13 — relevant, quality information).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

Records created or imported without the required field populated, or carrying a value outside the permitted domain.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Incomplete or invalid master/transaction data propagates downstream — mis-posting, failed interfaces, and unreliable reporting.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Make the field mandatory at entry, validate the domain, and remediate the existing exceptions.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerGroup Controller / CFO
SupportingFinancial Reporting Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Make the field mandatory at entry, validate the domain, and remediate the existing exceptions.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Strengthen period-close discipline: configure automatic period-lock at end-of-month + 5 working days; require dual-approval for any period reopen with documented business reason; route every manual JE above materiality through a 4-eyes approval workflow; weekly suspense / clearing account reconciliation with aged > 30-day escalation.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Zero closed-period postings in next cycle; trial balance foots daily; period-close completes within 5 working days of month-end. TB-balance check automated as part of close checklist.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

9 · Reproduction

Enforce the foreign-key relationship; restore or create the missing master records; investigate the create process.

3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Strengthen period-close discipline: configure automatic period-lock at end-of-month + 5 working days; require dual-approval for any period reopen with documented business reason; route every manual JE above materiality through a 4-eyes approval workflow; weekly suspense / clearing account reconciliation with aged > 30-day escalation.

9 · Reproduction

SELECT * FROM CUST.GACCENTRYD;
10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 27,432 rows (click to expand)
F-R2R-004

Temporary 'actual' GL entry not finalized within 30 days

MEDIUM Record-to-Report Source: GL-F3-001 2,099 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Existence Completeness Accuracy Cutoff Classification Presentation

1 · Observation

2,099 exception(s) detected. Long-lived temporary (STA_0=1) actual (CAT_0=1) entries are unposted to the official ledger yet affect working balances; auditors flag aged temporaries. The large RPS01 count is an artefact of a static demo where almost all entries remain temporary and every date is in the past — re-baseline this on live data with a rolling window..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Document status flags must be internally consistent and progress through valid states (header ↔ line ↔ downstream).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A status combination arose that the workflow should not permit — e.g. a long-lived temporary entry, or a line with no accounting value.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Inconsistent status drives wrong inclusion/exclusion in reporting and can mask unposted or stuck transactions.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Reconcile the status flags, finalise or reverse the stuck documents, and add a status-transition control.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerGroup Controller / CFO
SupportingFinancial Reporting Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Reconcile the status flags, finalise or reverse the stuck documents, and add a status-transition control.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Strengthen period-close discipline: configure automatic period-lock at end-of-month + 5 working days; require dual-approval for any period reopen with documented business reason; route every manual JE above materiality through a 4-eyes approval workflow; weekly suspense / clearing account reconciliation with aged > 30-day escalation.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Zero closed-period postings in next cycle; trial balance foots daily; period-close completes within 5 working days of month-end. TB-balance check automated as part of close checklist.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

9 · Reproduction

SELECT * FROM CUST.GACCENTRY;
10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 2,099 rows (click to expand)
F-R2R-005

Validation date must not precede the accounting date

MEDIUM Record-to-Report Source: GL-F3-002 39 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Existence Completeness Accuracy Cutoff Classification Presentation

1 · Observation

39 exception(s) detected. A validation timestamp earlier than the accounting date is logically impossible and signals a back-dated or mis-keyed document. 40 RPS01 rows genuinely violate it..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Document status flags must be internally consistent and progress through valid states (header ↔ line ↔ downstream).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A status combination arose that the workflow should not permit — e.g. a long-lived temporary entry, or a line with no accounting value.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Inconsistent status drives wrong inclusion/exclusion in reporting and can mask unposted or stuck transactions.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Reconcile the status flags, finalise or reverse the stuck documents, and add a status-transition control.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerGroup Controller / CFO
SupportingFinancial Reporting Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Reconcile the status flags, finalise or reverse the stuck documents, and add a status-transition control.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Strengthen period-close discipline: configure automatic period-lock at end-of-month + 5 working days; require dual-approval for any period reopen with documented business reason; route every manual JE above materiality through a 4-eyes approval workflow; weekly suspense / clearing account reconciliation with aged > 30-day escalation.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Zero closed-period postings in next cycle; trial balance foots daily; period-close completes within 5 working days of month-end. TB-balance check automated as part of close checklist.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

9 · Reproduction

SELECT * FROM CUST.GACCENTRY;
10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 39 rows (click to expand)
F-R2R-006

A matched (lettered) group on the legal ledger must net to zero

MEDIUM Record-to-Report Source: GL-F4-001 9 row(s) tested · sample 9
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Existence Completeness Accuracy Cutoff Classification Presentation

1 · Observation

9 exception(s) detected. Matching/lettering ties an invoice to its payment(s); a matched group that does not net to zero means an unbalanced or partial match was lettered as complete — a reconciliation defect. 49 RPS01 groups genuinely fail (MTC_0 is the match code per account+partner)..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Purchase order, receipt and invoice must agree on quantity and price (the classic 3-way match — SOX 404 P2P control).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

Quantities or amounts diverge across the matched documents beyond tolerance.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Over- or under-billing, duplicate payment, and unrecorded liabilities.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Investigate the mismatched documents, recover or accrue the difference, and enforce match tolerances at invoice posting.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerGroup Controller / CFO
SupportingFinancial Reporting Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Investigate the mismatched documents, recover or accrue the difference, and enforce match tolerances at invoice posting.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Strengthen period-close discipline: configure automatic period-lock at end-of-month + 5 working days; require dual-approval for any period reopen with documented business reason; route every manual JE above materiality through a 4-eyes approval workflow; weekly suspense / clearing account reconciliation with aged > 30-day escalation.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Zero closed-period postings in next cycle; trial balance foots daily; period-close completes within 5 working days of month-end. TB-balance check automated as part of close checklist.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

10 · Sample evidence — 9 of 9 rows (click to expand)
F-R2R-007

Currency with no human-readable label

MEDIUM Record-to-Report Source: MD-F1-001 114 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Existence Completeness Accuracy Cutoff Classification Presentation

1 · Observation

114 exception(s) detected. 115/163 currencies have both long (CURDES_0) and short (CURSHO_0) descriptions blank (single-space placeholder) — the minor/exotic ISO currencies. Existing IS-NOT-NULL rule passes vacuously; blank-or-space is the honest test.. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Mandatory fields and valid domain values are required for every record (data-integrity baseline; COSO Principle 13 — relevant, quality information).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

Records created or imported without the required field populated, or carrying a value outside the permitted domain.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Incomplete or invalid master/transaction data propagates downstream — mis-posting, failed interfaces, and unreliable reporting.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Make the field mandatory at entry, validate the domain, and remediate the existing exceptions.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerGroup Controller / CFO
SupportingFinancial Reporting Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Make the field mandatory at entry, validate the domain, and remediate the existing exceptions.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Strengthen period-close discipline: configure automatic period-lock at end-of-month + 5 working days; require dual-approval for any period reopen with documented business reason; route every manual JE above materiality through a 4-eyes approval workflow; weekly suspense / clearing account reconciliation with aged > 30-day escalation.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Zero closed-period postings in next cycle; trial balance foots daily; period-close completes within 5 working days of month-end. TB-balance check automated as part of close checklist.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale

F-R2R-008

VAT code with blank direct description

MEDIUM Record-to-Report Source: MD-F1-006 55 row(s) tested · sample 0
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Existence Completeness Accuracy Cutoff Classification Presentation

1 · Observation

55 exception(s) detected. 393/426 VAT codes have blank direct VATDES_0 BUT all 426 carry a populated localized label in LANDESSHO_0. Description not truly missing — lives in the language-keyed column. Production control should read LANDESSHO_0, not VATDES_0.. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Mandatory fields and valid domain values are required for every record (data-integrity baseline; COSO Principle 13 — relevant, quality information).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

Records created or imported without the required field populated, or carrying a value outside the permitted domain.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Incomplete or invalid master/transaction data propagates downstream — mis-posting, failed interfaces, and unreliable reporting.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Make the field mandatory at entry, validate the domain, and remediate the existing exceptions.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

SELECT * FROM CUST.TABVAT;
10 · Sample evidence — 0 of 55 rows (click to expand)
F-R2R-009

Active currency with no exchange rate on file

MEDIUM Record-to-Report Source: MD-F2-001 134 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Existence Completeness Accuracy Cutoff Classification Presentation

1 · Observation

134 exception(s) detected. An active currency with no row in TABCHANGE (as base CURDEN_0 or quote CUR_0) cannot value a FX transaction or revalue an open balance. 134/163 lack a rate — expected for the long tail the demo never trades in.. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Every foreign reference (third-party, account, parent document) must resolve to an existing master record (COSO Principle 10 — control activities over completeness).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A record references a master or parent that does not exist in the dataset — an orphan.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Orphans break reconciliations, hide liabilities/receivables, and indicate a broken create-sequence or an incomplete master file.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Enforce the foreign-key relationship; restore or create the missing master records; investigate the create process.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerGroup Controller / CFO
SupportingFinancial Reporting Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceQuarterly
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 30 days Quantify exposure (count × amount) and identify the responsible process step. Document the remediation plan with the process owner and add to the next audit-committee tracker.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 90 days Enforce the foreign-key relationship; restore or create the missing master records; investigate the create process.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 180 days Strengthen period-close discipline: configure automatic period-lock at end-of-month + 5 working days; require dual-approval for any period reopen with documented business reason; route every manual JE above materiality through a 4-eyes approval workflow; weekly suspense / clearing account reconciliation with aged > 30-day escalation.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Quarterly Zero closed-period postings in next cycle; trial balance foots daily; period-close completes within 5 working days of month-end. TB-balance check automated as part of close checklist.

VAT code with no rate row

MEDIUM Record-to-Report Source: MD-F2-002 48 row(s) tested · sample 0
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Partially Effective
Residual risk Medium
Audit assertions tested Existence Completeness Accuracy Cutoff Classification Presentation

1 · Observation

48 exception(s) detected. A VAT code with no entry in the time-phased rate table (TABRATVAT) cannot resolve a rate at posting time. Some may be intentionally rate-less (exempt/structure codes).. (scope-sensitive — this control tests against a master/parent table provided only in part in this extract; the count predominantly reflects extract completeness rather than a confirmed source-system failure)

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Every foreign reference (third-party, account, parent document) must resolve to an existing master record (COSO Principle 10 — control activities over completeness).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A record references a master or parent that does not exist in the dataset — an orphan.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Orphans break reconciliations, hide liabilities/receivables, and indicate a broken create-sequence or an incomplete master file.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Enforce the foreign-key relationship; restore or create the missing master records; investigate the create process.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

SELECT * FROM CUST.TABVAT;
10 · Sample evidence — 0 of 48 rows (click to expand)
F-ITGC-011

Enabled account that has never actually logged in

LOW IT General Controls Source: SY-F8-009 6 row(s) tested · sample 6
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Largely Effective
Residual risk Low
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

6 exception(s) detected. 64 of 81 enabled accounts have no ALOGIN session in this dataset. Overstates on a partial extract (ALOGIN holds only 14 distinct users) — corroborate with DATCONN_0. Dormant-but-enabled accounts are a standard de-provisioning finding..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Incompatible duties must be segregated and access appropriately restricted (COSO Principle 10/11; SOX ITGC).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A user holds an incompatible combination of rights, or an over-privileged / orphaned account exists.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

A single individual can both initiate and conceal a transaction — the core fraud-enablement condition.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Remove the conflicting access or add a compensating review; disable orphaned/all-access accounts; re-certify access.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerChief Information Officer
SupportingIT Security Lead · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceSemi-annual
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 60 days Monitor the metric on the next audit cycle. Define a tolerance threshold; promote to MEDIUM if the metric does not trend toward zero within two cycles.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 120 days Remove the conflicting access or add a compensating review; disable orphaned/all-access accounts; re-certify access.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 365 days Enforce 90-day password rotation OR (preferred) adopt MFA + SAML SSO; configure automatic deactivation of accounts after 90 days of inactivity; document and operationalise the SoD matrix covering ≥ 15 high-risk pairs; quarterly access-rights attestation by line managers with archived evidence.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Semi-annual Zero dormant active accounts; SoD matrix coverage ≥ 15 high-risk pairs with zero matrix-violation user-rights records; password / MFA compliance ≥ 99% on the active user population.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

9 · Reproduction

SELECT * FROM CUST.AUTILIS;
10 · Sample evidence — 6 of 6 rows (click to expand)
F-P2P-014

Payment detail line amount must be non-zero

LOW Procure-to-Pay Source: TP-F1-006 11 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Largely Effective
Residual risk Low
Audit assertions tested Completeness Cutoff Valuation/Accuracy Rights & Obligations

1 · Observation

11 exception(s) detected. A zero-amount payment line carries no information and signals an incomplete allocation. Extends the existing kb_constraint to AMTLIN_0 (the verified line-amount column; the prior rule's AMTLIN_0 reference is the same)..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Mandatory fields and valid domain values are required for every record (data-integrity baseline; COSO Principle 13 — relevant, quality information).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

Records created or imported without the required field populated, or carrying a value outside the permitted domain.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Incomplete or invalid master/transaction data propagates downstream — mis-posting, failed interfaces, and unreliable reporting.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Make the field mandatory at entry, validate the domain, and remediate the existing exceptions.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerHead of Procurement
SupportingAP Manager · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceSemi-annual
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 within 60 days Monitor the metric on the next audit cycle. Define a tolerance threshold; promote to MEDIUM if the metric does not trend toward zero within two cycles.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 within 120 days Make the field mandatory at entry, validate the domain, and remediate the existing exceptions.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 within 365 days Lock down the upstream control: enforce vendor-onboarding due diligence (VIES VAT-ID lookup, bank-account verification with callback, AML / sanctions screening) at the parameter level. Configure the X3 workflow to require 4-eyes approval on every PO above the materiality threshold and to block self-approval. Enable the duplicate-invoice-number check (INVCTLREF=block).
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Semi-annual Exception volume reduced by ≥ 50% within 90 days; reached industry-baseline tolerance within 12 months. KPI tracked monthly with trend chart presented to Audit Committee quarterly.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open

GL legal-ledger line should not be empty (no amount and no quantity)

LOW Record-to-Report Source: GL-F3-004 3,397 row(s) tested · sample 10
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Largely Effective
Residual risk Low
Audit assertions tested Existence Completeness Accuracy Cutoff Classification Presentation

1 · Observation

3,397 exception(s) detected. A line with neither amount nor quantity carries no accounting information and clutters the ledger; the quantity carve-out (QTY_0=0) excludes legitimate statistical/quantity-only lines (36 such lines were excluded)..

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Document status flags must be internally consistent and progress through valid states (header ↔ line ↔ downstream).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A status combination arose that the workflow should not permit — e.g. a long-lived temporary entry, or a line with no accounting value.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Inconsistent status drives wrong inclusion/exclusion in reporting and can mask unposted or stuck transactions.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Reconcile the status flags, finalise or reverse the stuck documents, and add a status-transition control.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

9 · Reproduction

SELECT * FROM CUST.GACCENTRYD;
10 · Sample evidence — 10 of 3,397 rows (click to expand)
F-ITGC-012

Table flagged for auditing but with no field-level audit configuration

CLEAN IT General Controls Source: SY-F10-002 0 row(s) tested · sample 0
▾
Inherent risk High
Control effectiveness Effective
Residual risk Low
Audit assertions tested Access Change Management IT Operations

1 · Observation

No exceptions — this control was tested over 100% of the population and passed.

2 · Criteria · what should be (control objective)

Sensitive changes must be logged and attributable (audit-trail completeness; SOX ITGC).

3 · Cause · why the exception arose

A sensitive table lacks change auditing, or a change is not attributable to a known user.

4 · Effect · business and financial impact

Without an audit trail, unauthorised changes cannot be detected or investigated.

6 · Auditor recommendation — what should be done

Enable change auditing on the sensitive tables and ensure every change is attributable to an authenticated user.

7 · Remediation plan — structured 4-phase response

Suggested ownerChief Information Officer
SupportingIT Security Lead · Internal Audit
Monitoring cadenceAnnual reperformance
Phase Timeline Action
1 · Containment
Stop the bleeding		 not applicable No containment required — control is effective on the dimensions tested. Maintain the existing control activities; reperform per the validation cadence below to confirm continued effectiveness.
2 · Root-cause remediation
Fix the underlying issue		 not applicable Enable change auditing on the sensitive tables and ensure every change is attributable to an authenticated user.
3 · Preventive control
Stop it recurring		 not applicable Enforce 90-day password rotation OR (preferred) adopt MFA + SAML SSO; configure automatic deactivation of accounts after 90 days of inactivity; document and operationalise the SoD matrix covering ≥ 15 high-risk pairs; quarterly access-rights attestation by line managers with archived evidence.
4 · Validation & monitoring
Prove it worked		 Annual reperformance Zero dormant active accounts; SoD matrix coverage ≥ 15 high-risk pairs with zero matrix-violation user-rights records; password / MFA compliance ≥ 99% on the active user population.

8 · Management response — to be completed at sign-off

Response Pending — management to indicate Accept · Partially Accept · Reject + rationale
Owner To be assigned
Target remediation date To be agreed
Status Open
